title: Explain event delegation in JavaScript
question: What is event delegation? Why is it useful? Give a practical example.
answer:
Event delegation is a pattern where a single listener attached to a parent element handles events for many child elements, using event.target to identify the origin.

Why it's useful:

  • Fewer listeners → less memory, faster setup.
  • Works for elements added after the listener was attached.
document.getElementById('list').addEventListener('click', (e) => {
  const li = e.target.closest('li');
  if (!li) return;
  console.log('clicked:', li.dataset.id);
});

title: What does the `key` prop do in React lists?
question: Why does React require a key prop on list items? What happens if you use the array index as the key?
answer:
React uses key to identify which items changed, were added, or were removed between renders. A stable, unique key lets React reuse DOM nodes instead of recreating them.

Using the array index works for static lists but breaks as soon as items are reordered, inserted, or removed — React will mismatch state (like input values or focus) with the wrong item.

// ❌ index as key: buggy when list reorders
{items.map((item, i) => )}

// ✅ stable id as key
{items.map((item) => )}

title: Implement a debounce function
question: Write a debounce(fn, delay) utility. Calling the returned function repeatedly should only invoke fn once delay ms have passed with no further calls.
answer:
function debounce(fn, delay) {
  let timer;
  return function (...args) {
    clearTimeout(timer);
    timer = setTimeout(() => fn.apply(this, args), delay);
  };
}

// Usage
const onResize = debounce(() => console.log('done'), 200);
window.addEventListener('resize', onResize);

Key points interviewers look for:

  • Preserving this and all args.
  • Clearing the previous timer on every call.
  • Bonus: add a .cancel() method, or a leading edge option.

title: Difference between `useMemo` and `useCallback`
question: When would you reach for useMemo vs useCallback? Are they ever interchangeable?
answer:
Both memoize across renders; the difference is what they memoize.

  • useMemo(fn, deps) → caches the return value of fn.
  • useCallback(fn, deps) → caches the function itself. Equivalent to useMemo(() => fn, deps).
const sorted = useMemo(() => heavySort(items), [items]);
const onClick = useCallback(() => setOpen(true), []);

Reach for them to stabilize references passed to memoized children or effect deps — not as a default for every value.

title: How does CSS specificity work?
question: Explain CSS specificity. Given #nav .item vs ul li.item, which wins and why?
answer:
Specificity is a 4-tuple (inline, ids, classes/attrs/pseudo-classes, elements/pseudo-elements), compared left-to-right.

  • #nav .item → (0, 1, 1, 0)
  • ul li.item → (0, 0, 1, 2)

ID beats any number of classes/elements, so #nav .item wins.

Tie-breakers: when specificity is equal, source order wins (last rule). !important overrides normal cascade but should be a last resort.

title: What is the event loop?
question:
Walk through what happens when this code runs:

console.log('a');
setTimeout(() => console.log('b'), 0);
Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log('c'));
console.log('d');
answer:
Output: a, d, c, b.

  1. Synchronous code runs first → a, d.
  2. Microtasks drain before the next task → promise callback logs c.
  3. Then the timer task fires → b.

The event loop picks one task, runs it to completion, drains all microtasks, then repeats. setTimeout(..., 0) is a task; promise .then callbacks are microtasks and always run first.

title: Flatten a deeply nested array
question: Implement flatten(arr) that flattens an arbitrarily nested array. Don't use Array.prototype.flat(Infinity).
answer:
function flatten(arr) {
  const out = [];
  for (const x of arr) {
    if (Array.isArray(x)) out.push(...flatten(x));
    else out.push(x);
  }
  return out;
}

// Iterative version (no recursion limit):
function flattenIter(arr) {
  const stack = [...arr];
  const out = [];
  while (stack.length) {
    const x = stack.pop();
    if (Array.isArray(x)) stack.push(...x);
    else out.unshift(x);
  }
  return out;
}

title: Explain `useEffect` dependency array pitfalls
question: What goes wrong when the useEffect dependency array is missing values it reads? Omitted entirely?
answer:
Missing a dep → the effect captures stale values from an earlier render (stale closure), causing subtle bugs: wrong prop read, old state, outdated fetch.

Omitting the array entirely → the effect runs on every render, often triggering infinite loops if the effect also sets state.

Empty array [] → runs once on mount. Safe only when the effect truly reads nothing reactive.

// ❌ stale userId if it ever changes
useEffect(() => { fetchUser(userId); }, []);

// ✅
useEffect(() => { fetchUser(userId); }, [userId]);

Use the exhaustive-deps ESLint rule — it catches 90% of these.

title: Difference between `==` and `===`
question: What's the difference between == and === in JavaScript? When is == ever okay?
answer:
• === (strict equality) compares value and type, no coercion.
  • == (loose equality) coerces operands to a common type first, per a messy algorithm.
0 == '';      // true   (both become 0)
0 == '0';     // true
null == undefined; // true
NaN == NaN;   // false

0 === '';     // false

The one idiomatic use of == is x == null, which is true for both null and undefined. Otherwise default to ===.

title: How does CSS Flexbox differ from Grid?
question: When do you reach for Flexbox vs CSS Grid? Give a concrete use case for each.
answer:
Flexbox is one-dimensional — distribute items along a single axis. Grid is two-dimensional — align items to rows and columns simultaneously.

  • Flex: navbar with logo + links + actions, a row of cards, centering one element.
  • Grid: page layout (header / sidebar / main / footer), image galleries, dashboards with precise alignment across rows and columns.
/* Flex: one row, space apart */
.nav { display: flex; justify-content: space-between; }

/* Grid: 2D dashboard */
.app { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 240px 1fr; grid-template-rows: 56px 1fr; }

title: What is the critical rendering path?
question: Explain the critical rendering path. What are the biggest levers for improving first paint?
answer:
The critical rendering path is the steps the browser takes from receiving HTML to painting pixels:

  1. Parse HTML → DOM
  2. Parse CSS → CSSOM (blocks rendering)
  3. Combine into render tree
  4. Layout (geometry)
  5. Paint + composite

Biggest first-paint levers:

  • Inline critical CSS, defer the rest.
  • async / defer on scripts so they don't block HTML parsing.
  • Preconnect / preload for fonts and hero images.
  • Reduce CSS size — CSSOM blocks rendering.
  • Server push or HTTP/2 for parallelization.

title: Implement `Promise.all` from scratch
question: Implement your own promiseAll(promises) with the same semantics as Promise.all.
answer:
function promiseAll(promises) {
  return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
    const results = new Array(promises.length);
    let remaining = promises.length;
    if (remaining === 0) return resolve(results);
    promises.forEach((p, i) => {
      Promise.resolve(p).then(
        (value) => {
          results[i] = value;
          if (--remaining === 0) resolve(results);
        },
        reject, // first rejection wins
      );
    });
  });
}

Gotchas: use Promise.resolve(p) to handle non-thenables, preserve order (by index), and short-circuit on first rejection.

title: What is semantic HTML and why does it matter?
question: Give examples of semantic vs non-semantic HTML. Why does it matter for accessibility and SEO?
answer:
Semantic HTML uses tags that describe meaning (<nav>, <article>, <button>, <header>). Non-semantic equivalents like <div> / <span> carry no meaning.

  • Accessibility: screen readers announce landmarks and roles correctly — users can jump between sections, activate buttons with Enter/Space, etc. A <div onClick> fails all of that.
  • SEO: search engines weight headings (h1–h6) and structural elements to understand the page.
  • Default behavior: <button> is focusable and fires on Enter/Space for free.

title: What's a closure? Show a classic gotcha.
question: Define a closure. Explain the classic var-in-a-loop bug and how let fixes it.
answer:
A closure is a function that remembers the variables from the scope in which it was defined, even after that scope has exited.

// ❌ All three log 3 — `i` is shared, final value captured.
for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
  setTimeout(() => console.log(i), 0);
}

// ✅ `let` creates a fresh binding per iteration → 0, 1, 2.
for (let i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
  setTimeout(() => console.log(i), 0);
}

var is function-scoped, so all timers close over the same i. let is block-scoped — each iteration gets its own binding.

title: How do you make a component accessible?
question: What are the top things you check to make a custom interactive component (e.g. a dropdown) accessible?
answer:
• Semantic root: use native <button> / <select> when possible.
  • Keyboard: Tab to focus, Enter/Space to activate, Arrow keys to navigate options, Esc to close.
  • Focus: visible focus ring; when opening, move focus into the menu; when closing, return it to the trigger.
  • ARIA: role="menu"/listbox", aria-expanded, aria-activedescendant, aria-labelledby.
  • Screen reader: test with VoiceOver/NVDA, not just visually.
  • Color contrast: WCAG AA (4.5:1 for text).
